For all other callbacks, the enums shall be a negative number indicating the task
`ScrollView` needs to allow `main.cpp` to register callbacks:

user_prompt: `ScrollView` is doing constant full screen refreshes even when not being interacted with, this is bad, it shouldn't do that
hold on, this means the current logic is that a view is dirty if items are dirty? no, those concepts should be decoupled globally
agent_reply: Yes, exactly. Globally, `FlyGuiView::dirty` and `FlyGuiItem::dirty` should stay decoupled.

The mistake was local to `ScrollView`: its override was treating item dirtiness as a reason to repaint the whole scroll view. That was especially bad because offscreen generated items can remain dirty, so the view kept full-refreshing forever.
I changed `ScrollView::redraw()` so it only full redraws when:
I also kept the `FlyGuiItem::setFaded()` fix so calling `setFaded(true)` repeatedly does not keep marking the item dirty when nothing changed.
I did not rerun the build after your stop.
Yes, exactly. Globally, `FlyGuiView::dirty` and `FlyGuiItem::dirty` should stay decoupled.

The mistake was local to `ScrollView`: its override was treating item dirtiness as a reason to repaint the whole scroll view. That was especially bad because offscreen generated items can remain dirty, so the view kept full-refreshing forever.
I changed `ScrollView::redraw()` so it only full redraws when:
I also kept the `FlyGuiItem::setFaded()` fix so calling `setFaded(true)` repeatedly does not keep marking the item dirty when nothing changed.
I did not rerun the build after your stop.

agent_reply: Fixed the likely flicker source in [ScrollView.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/ScrollView/ScrollView.cpp:462).

The hidden delete button could stay dirty after leaving delete mode, and `ScrollView::redraw()` was treating that as a reason to clear/redraw the whole view every frame. That also explains why delete mode itself looked fine: while visible, the delete button redraws and becomes clean.

- Explicitly clear the delete item’s dirty flag when it is hidden.

user_prompt: I am asking for a big change to both `WifiManager` and `BtHostList`
Previously, the WiFi credentials lived in a JSON file on the microSD card. This is of course vulnerable.
I want all configurations to live inside NVS when the build has security enabled
I have started to sprinkle the flag "BUILD_WITH_SECURITY" in a lot of places.

So instead of a linked list that is loaded from JSON, we will use structures with a fixed number of entries. The strings are fixed length instead of allocated on demand. 8 entries for everything should be more than enough.
* make sure the string lengths make sense for the data it needs to hold
* add constants for the string lengths so the code doesn't have hard coded numbers
* implement the loading and saving of the structures through NVS
* make everything is still compatible with current code

The bluetooth part of this has some added features, like a user customized name vs reported name storage, and a last used time. For last used time, only if the storage is full and pairing happens, the oldest unbonded entry is overwritten. If all entries are bonded, then the oldest bonded entry is overwritten.

there shall be two implementations of `loadFromMicroSd`, with `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY`, it loads the JSON but it loads it into NVS if existing, if not existing, no error is shown. Without `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY`, it loads as it does now.
agent_reply: Implemented the secure NVS-backed paths for both managers.
- [WifiManager.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.h:7) now defines fixed string-length constants and 8-slot secure storage for stations, APs, and cloud endpoints.

- [WifiManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp:765) now loads NVS first under `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY`, optionally imports `/wifi.json` into NVS if present, and treats missing JSON as non-fatal.

- [BtHostList.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BtHostList.h:8) now defines fixed Bluetooth host storage, including display name, custom name, reported name, bond state, last-used time, and 8 slots.
- [BtHostList.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BtHostList.cpp:444) now imports `/bluetooth.json` into NVS under `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY`, with missing JSON non-fatal.

- [BtHostList.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BtHostList.cpp:797) now supports secure fixed-slot insert/remove. If full, pairing overwrites oldest unbonded first, otherwise oldest bonded.
- Kept compatibility fields like `ssid`, `password`, `name`, and `next_node` so current UI/test/main code still works.
- Normal `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest` compiles touched C++ and still fails only at the existing IRAM overflow: `.iram0.text` overflowed by `2052 bytes`.

- Direct secure-branch compile with `-DBUILD_WITH_SECURITY` passed for `WifiManager.cpp`, `BtHostList.cpp`, `main.cpp`, `CloudUpload.cpp`, `ScrollView.cpp`, `BluetoothManager.cpp`, and `test_bootreadfiles.cpp`.
Implemented the secure NVS-backed paths for both managers.
- [WifiManager.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.h:7) now defines fixed string-length constants and 8-slot secure storage for stations, APs, and cloud endpoints.

- [WifiManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp:765) now loads NVS first under `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY`, optionally imports `/wifi.json` into NVS if present, and treats missing JSON as non-fatal.

- [BtHostList.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BtHostList.h:8) now defines fixed Bluetooth host storage, including display name, custom name, reported name, bond state, last-used time, and 8 slots.
- [BtHostList.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BtHostList.cpp:444) now imports `/bluetooth.json` into NVS under `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY`, with missing JSON non-fatal.

- [BtHostList.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BtHostList.cpp:797) now supports secure fixed-slot insert/remove. If full, pairing overwrites oldest unbonded first, otherwise oldest bonded.

- Normal `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest` compiles touched C++ and still fails only at the existing IRAM overflow: `.iram0.text` overflowed by `2052 bytes`.

- Direct secure-branch compile with `-DBUILD_WITH_SECURITY` passed for `WifiManager.cpp`, `BtHostList.cpp`, `main.cpp`, `CloudUpload.cpp`, `ScrollView.cpp`, `BluetoothManager.cpp`, and `test_bootreadfiles.cpp`.

user_prompt: This list needs to work with the bonded devices list (esp_bt_gap_get_bond_device_list)
If an entry exists in BtHostList but not esp_bt_gap_get_bond_device_list, it is kept for showing in the web front-end, but it does not show in the GUI ScrollView
If an entry exists in esp_bt_gap_get_bond_device_list but not in BtHostList, it does not show up on the web front-end nor the GUI ScrollView

On boot, and on submission of new configuration from web front-end, if an entry exists in esp_bt_gap_get_bond_device_list but not in BtHostList, then delete the bond with esp_bt_gap_remove_bond_device
Upon completion of bonding/pairing, an entry is entered into BtHostList
If the user edits a BDADDR to all 0 or blank in the web front-end, it means when it is submitted back, it is considered not a valid entry.
If the user selects to unpair a device through the touchscreen GUI, unpair it by deleting the bond, but do not remove the entry from BtHostList, as we might want to keep the customized name
Entries that are considered blank are never sent to the web front-end via the get_cfg request

Entries that are considered blank are never sent from the web front-end via the set_cfg request, but if encountered, they are skipped and do not enter the table, and that request will blank out the remainder of the table after processing the available JSON entries
I just wrote this policy, make sure the code follows this, do not remove the comment as the javascript side isn't implemented and I need to remember what the policy is
agent_reply: Implemented the policy and kept your comment in [BtHostList.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BtHostList.h:4).
- Boot now calls `bt_host_list->pruneBonds()` after loading, so bonded devices not present in `BtHostList` are removed.
- Web config submission now prunes bonds through `replaceHostList()`, so omitted hosts also lose their bond.
- GUI unpair now calls a new `BtHostList::unpair()` instead of `remove()`, so the bond is deleted but the host entry/custom name stays.
- `getFirstPhone()` / `getFirstLaptop()` now only return bonded entries, matching the GUI visibility policy.
- `/set_cfg` Bluetooth parsing now skips blank or all-zero MAC entries, blanks the remainder of the staged table, and still rejects malformed nonblank MACs.
- `/get_cfg` defensively skips blank Bluetooth slots if storage ever gets weird.
- `sanitize_bt_host_list()` compacts valid entries and blanks the tail.
Build passed, and I restored the generated `src/version.c` change afterward.
Implemented the policy and kept your comment in [BtHostList.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BtHostList.h:4).
- Boot now calls `bt_host_list->pruneBonds()` after loading, so bonded devices not present in `BtHostList` are removed.
- Web config submission now prunes bonds through `replaceHostList()`, so omitted hosts also lose their bond.
- GUI unpair now calls a new `BtHostList::unpair()` instead of `remove()`, so the bond is deleted but the host entry/custom name stays.
- `getFirstPhone()` / `getFirstLaptop()` now only return bonded entries, matching the GUI visibility policy.
- `/set_cfg` Bluetooth parsing now skips blank or all-zero MAC entries, blanks the remainder of the staged table, and still rejects malformed nonblank MACs.
- `/get_cfg` defensively skips blank Bluetooth slots if storage ever gets weird.
- `sanitize_bt_host_list()` compacts valid entries and blanks the tail.
Build passed, and I restored the generated `src/version.c` change afterward.

also, get rid of any code that deals with `legacy` nvs storage, we do not need to care about that until development has reached some form of release
agent_reply: Cleaned up the `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL` switch fallout and removed legacy NVS migration paths.
- Removed old `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY` branches and old linked-list compatibility paths from Wi-Fi/Bluetooth config code.
- Removed Wi-Fi and Bluetooth legacy NVS migration handling.
- Fixed fixed-entry structs fallout: removed `next_node` usage and cloud `name` usage.
- Level 0: `/get_cfg` and `/set_cfg` use plain JSON, cloud upload auth uses per-cloud password.
- Level 1+: config transport stays session encrypted, cloud upload auth uses `network-key`.
- FTP only starts when `BUILD_FTP_SERVER` is set and `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL <= 0`.
- Boot now loads Wi-Fi/Bluetooth JSON only for level 0; higher levels load NVS directly.
- Cloud list labels now use URL instead of removed cloud name.
- `git diff --check` on the files I edited passed.
- All four got through compilation and failed only at the known link-stage IRAM0 issue: `.iram0.text` overflowed by `2104 bytes`.

user_prompt: I know you just deleted it, but we need a `WifiApModeView` that is a `FlyGuiView`. This will display Wi-Fi credentials while in soft AP mode.
Place `sprit_wifi_50` in the top left corner (but not covering the top bar). If this is the default super safe randomly generated soft AP, then also put `sprite_security_50` beside it.

The bottom right corner, also linked to `BtnC`, put in a clickable `FlyGuiItem` that switches between `sprit_eye_closed_100` and `sprit_eye_open_100` when touched. Open eye means the info on the screen is displayed, closed means the sensitive parts are displayed as "**********"
Below the Wi-Fi icon, there should be 5 lines of text, similar to below
IP: <IP of the internal web server, probably 192.168.4.1>
Below the IP line, use the smallest font, show a line of text that cycles through a few displayed data items, cycling every 3 seconds, the items are:
* "page loads: n", number of times the "/get_info" request has been served since starting the AP
* "logins: n", number of times the "/get_cfg" request has been served since starting the AP, it is not displayed under security-level 0
* "saves: n", number of times the "/set_cfg" request (also the reset password and reset memory) has been served since starting the AP
* "errors: n", number of times there has been an apparent error, such as security-level mismatch error, or, some form of authentication error, those are the important ones
* "downloads: n", number of times a file has been downloaded, also track deletions and uploads with this number even though it doesn't make sense
`WifiManager` needs getters for these, and they need to be reset when the Wi-Fi starts, there may be request URL handlers missing but we'll implement those later

I am aware that I did not include some sort of stop button. The user must either click the physical reset button or power button to exit. So add a red line of text, small, as the next line, hinting so. I have no idea if it fits on the screen but do it so I can see.

user_prompt: Inside the `ScrollView` for Wi-Fi, the user can click on a `ScrollItem` representing a Wi-Fi router or initiate a scan-and-connect. This should lead to `ConnWaitingView`.
If the user cancels, the user should return to the `ScrollView` for Wi-Fi.
If the connection succeeds. There are two scenarios: at least one cloud upload destination is available, or none are available.

Create a `WifiStaModeView` much like `WifiApModeView`, borrow the same rough layout. There does not need to be a security icon. The SSID can always be shown in the same place and fonts and colour. The password is never shown. There does not need to be a show/hide button. The text should show “assigned IP:” and “gateway IP:”
The function where statistics are shown and cycled is kept.

If no cloud destinations are available, then `WifiStaModeView`  only shows the text about how you need to reset or power off to stop. Otherwise, there is a button `FlyViewItem` using `sprite_canceldoor_50` to dismiss the dialog to go back to the `ScrollView`, put this in the bottom right corner and tie it to `BtnC`.

Exiting out of the `ScrollView` for cloud items causes the microcontroller to reset.

When cloud destinations exist, it shows the `sprite_canceldoor_50` dismiss button on BtnC/bottom-right back to the cloud `ScrollView`; otherwise it shows the reset/power-off stop hint.

- Cancel disconnects Wi-Fi and returns to the Wi-Fi `ScrollView`.
- Successful station connect starts the web server, then shows cloud `ScrollView` if destinations exist, otherwise `WifiStaModeView`.

user_prompt: make it so that, even if Wi-Fi connection has failed or cancelled, clicking the back button inside the ScrollView for Wi-Fi items will cause the microcontroller to reset. Have some sort of Wifi-has-started indication from WifiManager to do this
agent_reply: Done. I added a sticky `WifiManager::wifiHasStarted()` flag in [WifiManager.h](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.h>) / [WifiManager.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp>) that flips true once STA connect, STA scan, or AP mode actually starts. It is intentionally not cleared by `disconnect()`, so cancel/fail still counts until reset.

Then [ScrollView](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/ScrollView/ScrollView.h>) exposes `isWifiContext()`, and [main.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp>) now resets when the ScrollView back button is pressed from Wi-Fi context after `wifiHasStarted()` is true.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`; build passed. Existing dirty sprite/check_dev_pace/version files were already in the worktree, and I left them alone.

commit_message: code neatened, remove cloud features with a predefine

user_prompt: it's working, kind of, device to host and host to device connections both work

pairing is a bad experience, I think it looked like it rebooted but I didn't see any messages at all in my terminal, the serial port is working, uh, maybe the confirmation dialog registered the wrong return view so it returned to the splash screen instead of the mainscreenview, make sure that's not possible

the press to hold for deleting pairing does work but there's no indication that the user held for long enough. I need the trash-can icon to show up as soon as the timespan for a long-hold has been reached, instead of being checked only on release.
it feels like I have to delete a pair twice before it disappears from the list.

I want you to do something different, you have prevented a connection after a pair, this makes the phone very unhappy. I want to go back to the way it was before, when we can always accept an incoming connection. We simply don't start a recording. I see `on_pairing_success_dialog_dismissed` already issues a reboot. That's good.
agent_reply: Implemented and verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`.
- Bluetooth now starts full HFP at boot again, so incoming host connections can be accepted normally.
- Pairing no longer deinitializes HFP or makes the radio non-connectable.
- Pairing mode suppresses auto-recording immediately, so a phone can connect after pairing without starting a recording.
- Pairing success dialog now falls back to `FLYGUI_VIEW_MAIN` before reboot, so it cannot land on splash if reboot is delayed or interrupted.
- Bluetooth long-hold delete now shows the trash icon as soon as the hold threshold is reached.
- Unpair no longer immediately saves through the path that re-reads bond state too early, which should fix the “delete twice before it disappears” behavior.
Build passes. One note: `pio` regenerated `src/version.c` during the build.

user_prompt: I have created `lib\WavPlayback\PlaybackView.cpp` and `lib\WavPlayback\WavPlayback.cpp` (and associated header files) to work with. `PlaybackView` should handle the GUI code as much as possible, while `WavPlayback` handles the reading of the file and the feeding of the audio into the speaker. Use `AudioManager` as much as possible. `PlaybackView` inherits from `FlyGuiView`
Clicking on a recorded file from the `ScrollView` will enter this view and start playback.
The duration of the file is an estimate based on file size, assuming 16 bit stereo PCM, and subtract the size of the RIFF header.
When playback is finished, go into pause mode (remember to change the icon).

use `lib\WavPlayback\ScrubBar.cpp` and the associated header file to implement a scrubbing bar much like the `ProgressBar` in the sense that we can define a size and position of it. It also needs initialization parameters for total time. It needs setters for current position. It needs a callback that is inside `PlaybackView`  to handle touch events on the bar so that the `PlaybackView`  can delegate the actual file seek to `WavPlayback` and then call the update function of `ScrubBar`.
At the right side of screen, just above the `ScrubBar` itself, show the total file duration as “hh:mm:ss”

At the left side of the screen, just below the `ScrubBar` itself, show the current position in the file as “hh:mm:ss”, but in yellow text, and the same slightly bigger font used for the elapsed time during recording. Even if the code does something weird, do not let the displayed current time number exceed the estimated duration.
The bar itself is always blue, and since it is now capable of going backwards, the drawing of the bar needs to also draw a filled black rectangle after the filled blue rectangle
`sprite_record_50` in the top left corner, below the top bar. Beside this icon, show the file name, with a normal font, center aligned horizontally with the 50x50 icon
(note: I know we can fit 3 lines of text here)
The region from just below the icon, which I think is Y = 60, this is where we put a 300 pixel wide 12 pixel tall `ScrubBar` , horizontally centered

At the bottom of the screen will be three buttons, each should be centered as if the screen was divided in 3 columns and then the buttons centered in the columns. The icons are also tied to `BtnA` `BtnB` and `BtnC`
The left button is a play/pause toggle control. Use `sprite_playback_play_50` and `sprite_playback_pause_50` as appropriate

The center button is volume control, which cycles through 4 settings, use `sprite_speaker_50` for full volume, `sprite_speaker_66_50` for 66% volume, `sprite_speaker_33_50` for 33% volume, and `sprite_speaker_00_50` for 0% volume.
The right button is one that stops playback and quits, use `sprite_squarex_50`
This needs to open the file, get its size (based on file size, not RIFF data), estimate the duration.
Every pump action is 0.5 second worth of data. Pump the data into the `g_fifo_bt2spk`, we are borrowing it. Enable the speaker through `AudioManager` and we should get sound.
There are setters for playback position, which simply sets the file seek pointer intelligently.
A setter is needed for volume, which should use the volume function already implemented by `AudioManager`, I am pretty sure the application of the gain is already implemented

A setter is needed for the play/pause state, pausing means no more reading file content and enqueuing and clearing of the FIFO. The file seek pointer needs to be re-wound a bit according to how much of the FIFO was cleared.

The pump function I will call from almost the same place that `AudioFileRecorder::pump();` happens in `main.cpp`, because this module is like the opposite of `AudioFileRecorder` and I want it on the same thread/core for the same reasons (SPI sharing for LCD screen and microSD card)

`WavPlayback` is started when `PlaybackView` is loaded, and stopped (file handle closed) when unloaded. I am pretty sure by doing it this way will mean that an incoming bluetooth connection will automatically stop the playback, close the file, and start a recording, which is great.
At this point, you can probably hand me something that is immediately testable. I already have a `.wav` file on the card.
I will tweak the UI, ask for bug fixes, and then see if I need to add more precise rewind and fast forwarding buttons.
agent_reply: - Added `WavPlayback` engine in [WavPlayback.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/WavPlayback.cpp): opens WAV, estimates duration from file size minus 44-byte RIFF header, seeks, pauses with FIFO rewind, sets volume, and pumps audio into `AudioManager::bluetoothToSpeakerFifo()`.
- Added `PlaybackView` in [PlaybackView.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/PlaybackView.cpp): file title, scrub bar, play/pause, 4-step volume, stop/exit.
- Wired `.wav` clicks from `ScrollView` into playback using the selected filename.
I also made playback mix stereo to mono before feeding the speaker FIFO, so overlapping mic/BT channels won’t silently drop the right channel.
Verification: `pio run -e thefly_sec0` passes. One intentional simplification: WAV parsing assumes the generated files are 16 kHz, stereo, 16-bit PCM with a 44-byte header, exactly as requested.

user_prompt: In the `ScrollView` currently any item representing an encrypted file is not clickable. Change it so that now, it launches the same playback mechanism, but only if `BUILD_WITH_ENCRYPTED_PLAYBACK` is defined

The playback mechanism, with `BUILD_WITH_ENCRYPTED_PLAYBACK` defined, can use `*.rec` files and have it use slightly different code paths that include decryption. The RIFF header is fixed size. The file size estimates can still be done with the available constants.
The seeking function will need to calculate the best actual file pointer for a given specified time. Do not bother going more precise than a chunk boundary.
agent_reply: `ScrollView` now routes `.rec` items through the existing playback callback only when `BUILD_WITH_ENCRYPTED_PLAYBACK` is defined: [ScrollView.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/ScrollView/ScrollView.cpp:1236).
- Decrypts the fixed-size encrypted RIFF header.
- Seeks to the nearest encrypted audio chunk boundary.
- Decrypts audio chunks on demand before mixing/queueing audio.

I exposed the recorder buffers through small accessors in [AudioFileRecorder.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.h:56), backed by the existing `g_wav_plaintext_audio` and `g_wav_encrypted_audio` buffers in [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:1283), so playback doesn’t allocate another large pair.
Both passed; the second sec1 build was after a final state-reset fix in the decrypt setup path.

user_prompt: We already have `WavPlayback`, and we have it in a namespace, turn it into a class object, with a base class `FilePlayback` and then `WavPlayback` will inherit from it. Also add `WavEncryptedPlayback` so that there is less branching on if statements.
Then implement `Mp3Playback` as another class that inherits `FilePlayback` , and in the same spirit, `Mp3EncryptedPlayback` as well

Whatever was using `WavPlayback` before will now simply use `FilePlayback` functions but decide if it constructs a `WavPlayback` or `Mp3Playback` or `WavEncryptedPlayback` or `Mp3EncryptedPlayback` based on file extension
Each one of these classes gets their own CPP and header file.

`Mp3EncryptedPlayback` and `WavEncryptedPlayback` should be inheriting from `Mp3Playback` and `WavPlayback` , and only offering decryption functions in between file reading and FIFO queuing, the file reading stuff and FIFO queueing should be inherited.
We retain the ability to playback `wav` files and `rec` files even if the MP3 compression is the recording method.

`PlaybackView` should not have to worry about file types, and handle seeking/scrubbing functions only based on time units. The playback class objects takes care of internally converting time units to file pointers.

For encrypted files, the time duration estimation only works with our defined sizes/lengths inside `inc\conf.h`, do not attempt to calculate file time duration by reading the actual file header, assume the encryption can only happen on files we record ourselves
For unencrypted files, the time duration estimation can come from just the header of the first MP3 frame. Assume it is constant bit rate.
I have placed `lib\libhelix-esp32-arduino` in the project, use `MP3DecoderHelix` for decoding as appropriate.
agent_reply: - Moved `PlaybackView` to construct playback by extension and talk only in time units through `FilePlayback`.
- Added Helix MP3 decode support, including a final sync probe so the last MP3 frame is flushed.
- Added `.mp3` / `.fly` to recent-file listing and disk stats while retaining `.wav` / `.rec`.
- Updated `src/main.cpp` to pump/stop through `PlaybackView` instead of static `WavPlayback` calls.
- `rg` found no remaining old `WavPlayback::` / namespace playback calls.
- `git diff --check` passes, aside from the repo’s CRLF warnings.

One note: `src/version.c` is dirty from the PlatformIO version generation step; I left it as-is. The build also still reports the existing ESP-FTP-Server `NetworkServer::available()` deprecation warning.

user_prompt: ensure users of `FileList get(size_t maxFiles)` isn't causing memory leaks
agent_reply: I audited the users. No memory leak found, and no caller changes were needed.
There are only two call sites:
- [ScrollView.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/ScrollView/ScrollView.cpp:500): keeps `FileList` on the stack, then passes each filename to `appendSpriteScrollItem`.
- [test_filelistbenchmark.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_filelistbenchmark.cpp:98): also keeps it on the stack.
The GUI path is safe because `ScrollItem::configureSprite()` copies the filename into its own `label_` buffer, so it does not retain pointers into the temporary `FileList`.

`FileList` itself is RAII-safe: destructor calls `reset()`, copy assignment deletes old storage after allocating replacement, and move assignment transfers ownership while nulling the source. `get()` also deletes its temporary `Candidate* recent` before returning.

user_prompt: also make sure every `.cpp` file inside `lib\FlyGuiViews` meet the guidelines at `developer-docs\code-layout-policy.md`, however, it is also important that their code layout look almost the same between them as they do kind of inherit from the same base classes sometimes

I also kept the FlyGui view files visually aligned with each other by using the same lightweight section markers and putting helper blocks at the bottom.

commit_message: scrollview uses a better return button

user_prompt: Create a file `lib\FlyGuiViews\ScrollView\FileScrollItem.cpp` and its associated header file.
It is configured with the file name

The file name (including extension) is hashed very quickly into a uint32_t number, this number should be deterministic based on the file name, and the purpose is so that the items in the `ScrollView` don’t all look identical.
For every file, based on the hash number, pick between `sprite_fileback_blue` or `sprite_fileback_yellow` as the backdrop of the icon. Both of these are 100x100 in size.

Now look at the file name, the starting letter should be an indicator of the recording context, such as a call/meeting, or memo, or some subtype of memo. These will pick a 50x50 icon to be overlayed on top of the background icon
For calls, meetings, plain memos, and unknowns, chose from:
Enforce a rule that is `sprite_fileback_yellow` can never be used together with `sprite_filetop_record`
There is no graphic indicator as to whether or not the file is encrypted or not, the file extension doesn’t matter in terms of visuals.
The drawing should also behave like before, where inactive items are drawn as faded. So simply draw both the background icon and the overlay icon in the same faded way.

Wire this into the `bool ScrollView::populateFiles()` and I am hoping this is easy. Firmware update and unknown file logic stay the same. (if I understand correctly, they won’t even show up now that we are filtering them out from the file list provider)
Please follow `developer-docs\code-layout-policy.md` when writing the code
agent_reply: Implemented `FileScrollItem` and wired it into the file scroll list.

Updated [ScrollView.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/ScrollView/ScrollView.cpp:250) so playable file entries use the new class, while firmware and unknown fallback sprites stay on the old path. The new item hashes the full filename, chooses blue/yellow backdrops, maps filename prefixes to the requested 50x50 overlays, and avoids the yellow + record combination. Existing faded drawing behavior is handled by the base sprite+overlay path.
- `pio run -e thefly_sec1` timed out after 5 minutes, but `FileScrollItem.cpp` did compile into that env before timeout.
- `git diff --check` passed for the tracked ScrollView edits.

user_prompt: Modify `MostRecentFiles` so that each `Candidate` contains a `file_size`, in bytes. And then the actual `FileList` also contains a `file_size` member.

`FileScrollItem` is initialized with knowledge about the `file_size`, and is responsible to calculate it into an estimated playback duration using the file size and file format. It generates a string internally as "<file size> / <duration HH:mm:SS>", and this can be read by `void ScrollView::drawSelectedText()` to be displayed underneath its label.
agent_reply: `MostRecentFiles` now carries byte sizes alongside filenames via `FileList::fileSize(index)`, populated from `FsFile::fileSize()` while building the recent-file list.
`FileScrollItem` now takes `fileSize` during `configureFile(...)`, formats an internal detail string like:
`ScrollView::drawSelectedText()` now keeps `scrollItem->label()` as the real filename, but renders the optional detail line underneath it when present.
`git diff --check` is clean except for the repo’s usual CRLF warning. `src/version.c` is still modified in the worktree; I left it alone.

user_prompt: point me at all the places where an intentional reset is about to happen
agent_reply: I found these whole-device intentional reset paths:
`requestRebootWithFlag(uint32_t flag)` sets `reset_magic` / `reset_flag`, waits 10 ms, then calls `ESP.restart()`. I did not find any current callers.
`reboot_after_reset_dialog()` logs “reset confirmation dismissed; rebooting”, delays, then `esp_restart()`. Used after password reset and memory reset dialogs.
When firmware update view is in `Complete` state, touch press/release delays then `esp_restart()`.
If cloud upload completes but modal/gui is unavailable, it logs “rebooting”, delays, then `esp_restart()`.
Exiting cloud scroll context reboots by policy after Wi-Fi/cloud use.
Exiting Wi-Fi scroll after Wi-Fi has started reboots by policy.
Cloud upload confirmation dismissed: logs, delays, `esp_restart()`.
Pairing confirmation dismissed: logs “rebooting to restart Bluetooth cleanly”, flushes serial, delays, `esp_restart()`.
One test intentionally expects a watchdog reset rather than directly calling restart:
Prints “WDT reset is expected” before running PBKDF without watchdog servicing.
I ignored ordinary “reset internal state” functions, FIFO resets, decoder resets, and `esp_task_wdt_reset()` because those are not whole-device resets.

`reboot_after_reset_dialog()` logs “reset confirmation dismissed; rebooting”, delays, then `esp_restart()`. Used after password reset and memory reset dialogs.
When firmware update view is in `Complete` state, touch press/release delays then `esp_restart()`.
If cloud upload completes but modal/gui is unavailable, it logs “rebooting”, delays, then `esp_restart()`.

I ignored ordinary “reset internal state” functions, FIFO resets, decoder resets, and `esp_task_wdt_reset()` because those are not whole-device resets.

user_prompt: use git diff, review how I added screen fades to places where I want the UI to appear at least responding to user input, sometimes masking a reboot, sometimes masking time consuming operations, and suggest other potential places
agent_reply: No blocking issues found in the fade additions.

The `PlaybackView::handleDelete()` addition is especially nice now that it also refreshes `DiskStats` after successful delete. That path can involve SD remove, disk-space scan, recording scan, and file-list repopulation, so a fade is a reasonable “I heard you” signal.
- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:220) `onclick_main_files()` before `scroll_view->populateFiles()`, especially if lots of files or security level 1 PIN path.
- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:236) `onclick_main_wifi()` before `scroll_view->populateWifi()`, if Wi-Fi list load/formatting can pause.

- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:247) smartphone/laptop connect buttons before `connect_to_bluetooth_host()`, since connection startup may feel dead before waiting view appears.

- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:430) `onclick_wifi_ap()` before AP/WebServer startup, because `WifiManager::startSoftAp()` plus `WebServer::init()` can be chunky.
- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:682) `onclick_ntp_sync()` before starting NTP, if setup can block before the waiting view.
- [ScrollView.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/ScrollView/ScrollView.cpp:906) Bluetooth host delete/unpair path, if `removeBond`/NVS save is perceptible.
Tiny style thought: if fades keep spreading, a named helper like `showBusyFeedback()` might make intent clearer than using `quickScreenFade()` directly everywhere. Not urgent.
